summary: Gruber critiques Mark Gurman's analogy comparing Apple's AI position to gas cars versus EVs, arguing it fundamentally misunderstands the relationship between AI and operating systems. He proposes a better analogy: AI is to today's OSes what web browsers were to Windows in the late 1990s — a new interactive layer atop existing platforms, not a replacement. Gruber traces Apple's history with Safari/WebKit to argue Apple needs an equivalent 'AI moment,' but worries about the timeline given how fast AI is moving. He draws a parallel to Microsoft's rapid response to Netscape, noting that speed of response determined who maintained platform dominance. The piece concludes by asking whether LLMs are more like browsers (must own) or web search (can profit from others'), suggesting the answer is somewhere in between and that Apple risks having to pay others rather than charging them.
key_points: Gurman's gas-car-to-EV analogy fails because AI tools run on top of existing platforms rather than replacing them — nobody uses ChatGPT instead of a phone or computer
The better analogy is web browsers in the 1990s: AI adds new interactive layers atop existing OSes, just as browsers did to Windows, without eliminating the underlying platform

Apple's Safari/WebKit history shows that owning primary technology is essential — it took Apple seven years after Netscape's IPO to ship Safari, and the question is whether Apple can afford a similar delay with AI
Microsoft survived the browser era because it quickly built a better browser than Netscape; Apple's AI fate depends on how fast it can reach competitive parity

Apple's Google search deal ($20B+/year) shows how platform control can generate enormous revenue even without owning the service — but LLMs may not follow this pattern since they run both locally and in the cloud
money_quotes: Gurman's analogy would only work if the way you got around in an EV was to put it in the back of a gas-powered flatbed truck.
AI is doing to today's dominant OSes what web browsers did to Windows in the late 1990s. They're adding new interactive layers atop the old.
They didn't just build a browser into Windows, they built a better browser into Windows.
The concern for Apple today is that they're in trouble if it takes six or seven years for them to get to their Safari/WebKit moment for AI.

If it takes Apple as long to have its own competitive LLMs as it did to have its own competitive web browser, I suspect they'll soon be paying to use the LLMs that are owned and controlled by others, not charging the others for the privilege of reaching Apple's platform users.
No simple analogy captures this dynamic. But the threat is palpable.
In hindsight, we now know that web browsers, in and of themselves, don't generate any money directly. Someone was going to give a good one away free and now almost all of them are free of charge.
I will say, though, 'Liquid Glass' sounds cool.
key_insight: Apple needs to achieve its 'Safari/WebKit moment' for AI far faster than the seven years it took with browsers, or it risks shifting from a platform that charges for integration to one that pays for access to others' AI.
